The Tomb of Nakhtamun at Thebes (TT 341)

VIII. The procession during the Beautiful Feast of the Valley

IX. Men celebrate the Beautiful Feast of the Valley

X. A floral ankh bouquet is presented to the nose of the deceased. Thank you to my husband, Joe Cain, for his patience during my absences while working on the text. I also extend my gratitude to Beatrice Rehl, Publishing Director at Cambridge University Press, Humanities and Social Sciences division, who encouraged me and showed good-humored patience throughout the longer-than-anticipated preparation of this manuscript.

Dynasty 5: 2497-2345 BC. The appearance of Pyramid Texts that explicate the king's afterlife. During Dynasty 25, Egypt was ruled by Nubian kings. Ptolemaic Period 332-30 BC. Following the death of Alexander the Great, Egypt was bequeathed to his general Ptolemy, after whom the Greek period in Egypt is named.

During the Beautiful Feast of the Valley, scenes of everyday activities were preserved for the afterlife. Procession took place showing statues of the deceased Amunhotep III and Queen Tiye. A floral ankh bouquet was presented to the nose of the deceased Pairy and his wife by their son during the celebration of the Feast of the Valley.

Anubis stands over a wrapped mummy, which is depicted with the four canopic jars on its left and oars to navigate the way to the afterlife on its right. This text does not delve into the myths of Osiris and Isis or Horus and Seth, as these stories are well-documented in numerous books. Instead, it explores the idea that there was no clear distinction between a "secular" realm in ancient Egyptian society, as all aspects of culture were deeply intertwined with religious practices.

There were few codified laws because the pharaoh was the highest judge in the land and all laws emanated from him. Although the Egyptians differentiated between good and bad art, their culture did not value art for its aesthetic appeal as it does in Western tradition. Statues played a crucial role in mortuary cults, as the Egyptians believed that a person could not exist in the afterlife unless their image – in the form of a statue – was preserved among the living.

The hieroglyphs, like statues, were replicas or counterimages of what they represented. People who held high social status were often recorded as holding specific titles, and it is estimated that a significant proportion of the population served in temples or participated in funerary cults. Although they functioned in a non-secular world, it's astonishing how similar their basic moral principles and life patterns are to ours today.

At least in legal affairs, women were considered equal to men – although exercising those rights socially was a more difficult matter; such legal equality has been achieved in only a few countries over the last half century. The Egyptians expressed grief at funerals and named children after their parents or grandparents. It is also important to be aware of the shortcomings of our documentation.

Our perception of what seems odd or contradictory can be misleading, as it's rooted in our unique perspective and understanding of the world. Our reality is shaped by a multitude of scientific concepts, some of which may appear irrational. In contrast, ancient Egyptians understood natural phenomena in tangible terms, eschewing speculative thinking. The sun did not cross the sky in a boat. People in the Nile Valley traveled by boat. They envisioned an afterlife modeled after their own lives, including everyday details such as food, household items, leisure activities.

Although the Egyptians were remarkably adept at simple computations using basic geometry, it was the Greeks – people of a distinctly different background and world outlook – who began to really question the world in theoretical terms. The Egyptians' apparent lack of curiosity about the world around them might seem like a intellectual dead end. However, it's striking and enlightening that their culture has endured for so long.

The world was fresh and perfect at the beginning of time, as the gods had created it. This perspective helps explain the remarkably faithful retention by the Egyptians of the earliest manifestations of their culture, such as the crowns and dress of their king, and the styles of their architecture and art, which emerged during the early Old Kingdom period. This framework provided the basis for their culture, with modest modifications, for three millennia. The persistence in emulating and championing the past was aimed at creating a safe and comforting environment.

We approach natural phenomena with an acceptance of abstractions that we cannot observe, such as molecules and atomic particles. In contrast, the ancient Egyptians explained their world in concrete terms, grounded in their observations of their surroundings. The scenes depicted in such settings are a common form of temple decoration, as they highlight the king's role as the primary link between humans and the divine. Ancient Egypt differed from the later Western world in not having monastic organizations. The ancient Egyptian priest was not expected to serve as an example of religiously dictated behavior.

Because of the lack of strictures on priestly behavior and the part-time nature of most priestly positions, identifying what characterizes a priest is challenging. In the mortuary context, the wab was responsible for carrying offerings in the funeral and thereafter for periodically supplying the tomb chapel with additional offerings. Despite their low rank, wab priests carried the sacred boat of the god during oracles.

This class of priest was considered literate and thus the keeper of specialized knowledge, referred to as seshta, often translated as "mysteries". To the right, an Egyptian priest, dressed in a simple kilt and sash, stands behind the statue of Seti I. A lector faces the statue. It was apparently a prestigious role, as suggested by the fact that Kaemwaset, a son of Ramses II, gave precedence to his priestly title over his title of "King's Son". The sem priest presided over the Opening of the Mouth ritual at a funeral, where he touched the face of the mummy with tools to revive the senses of the deceased in the afterlife.

During the reign of Ramesses III, records of the workmen's strike revealed a sem priest's unfortunate decision not to immediately grant the men their bread rations. Until recently, "hem netcher," meaning "God's Servant," was mistakenly translated as "prophet." This translation is now avoided due to its Judeo-Christian undertones. There were multiple Fourth and Third Priests at one time, but only two priests held the positions of Second and First Priest.

Nebwenenef, who ruled during Dynasty 19, was appointed as the first priest of Amun after previously serving as the first priest of Onuris in Dendera. He became the Third Priest, and then, after 15 more years, the Second Priest of Amun. He was finally appointed First Priest after twelve more years. He died after a career spanning nearly 70 years. Other priests followed a slower career path.

The text appears to be based on historical figures and events, but it contains several errors and inconsistencies. He was eventually appointed as the first priest of Amun. Women did not play an active role in ancient Egyptian temple establishments. Women generally held more prominent positions in the religious hierarchy during the Old Kingdom than they did in later periods. Women were almost exclusively singers (shamyet or heset) in divine choirs that accompanied priests during processions around and through the temple. The higher ranks followed the priests into the area near the sanctuary.

Among a group of 40 stelae dating to this period, only 11 lists a woman who bears a priestly title. Most of the women listed either have no title or are simply referred to as "Mistress of the House". The title "God's Wife" (also known as "hemet netcher," but referring to a different word than that for the priestess' title mentioned earlier) denotes a priestess of Amun during the Third Intermediate Period. It is first attested several times in the Middle Kingdom. There is no direct evidence to suggest that priestesses of any rank were celibate. Although little is known about their training, there is some information available on how priests were appointed.

Tutankhamun claimed that he installed priests "chosen from among the sons of local dignitaries and the children of men whose names were well-known." "A priest often inherited his position from his father, or at least he was considered eligible due to the family's familiarity with the duties."  Wool was not a commonly used fabric in earlier times. Linen was the preferred choice for clothing.

King Piye (Dynasty 25) said, "The rival kings and nobles of Lower Egypt couldn't enter the palace because they were uncircumcised. But Nimlot was allowed in because he was clean." "Although this quotation refers to a palace, the same strictures may have applied to the temple." Comparing these suggested prohibitions to the lists of foods offered to the god and subsequently returned to the temple staff reveals a different reality. The people of Oxyrrhynchus, who lived in a district marked by a fish emblem, clashed with those from Cynopolis, which was represented by a dog emblem. The latter had consumed oxyrhinque (fish) that had been seized from the former, and in retaliation, they killed and ate some dogs as victims.

One lives by the food of the gods. The term "food" refers to what comes from the offerings made to a deity after they have been appeased. "Sixty burnt offerings were not a common practice in Egypt." Priests were paid based on a formula that divided the total offerings presented in the temple or tomb by the number of days they served, rather than receiving a percentage of the food presented during their rotation. The system adjusted for the larger number of offerings typically presented during festivals when a particular priest was not available.

Thousands of years after they were built, these structures still possess the power and grandeur to awe the viewer. Pylons mark different sections of the temple. However, we know something about their appearance from texts and representations.

Although there were plenty of supplies of gold in the Nile Valley, silver was rare, and lapis lazuli was even more scarce, as it had to be imported from Afghanistan. Because of their literal-mindedness, the Egyptians described the Ba power of a god not in abstract terms, such as energy, but concretely, as a golden falcon that had the power to traverse from image to image, awakening each with the divine being. The text on the stela from Thutmose I states that the statue is less accessible than that which is in heaven, more secret than the affairs of the underworld, and more hidden than the inhabitants of the primeval ocean. "The gods, being modeled after humans, required sustenance for their physical existence and craved adornments."

The Egyptians believed that they could not take the presence of the god for granted. All ritual actions were performed in the name of the king, with him depicted in temple reliefs. In reality, a priest acted on behalf of the king. Following additional purifications, the priest removed the deity's outer linen garment and jewelry, then wiped away the unguents from the previous day's ritual. Finally, after further purifications, the god was returned to his shrine, with the doors closed and sealed.

The daily offering service was a fundamental part of Egyptian religion. It emerged during the Amarna Period, albeit in a slightly modified form, as described in Chapter 10. During this period, flowers became a more prominent feature of the offerings. It was believed that the Aten, as an abstract deity, could not consume food during sacrifices.

Gods often left their sanctuaries during festivals and for other purposes. These offering cults for the king were celebrated during his lifetime and were intended to continue long after his death. A few kings, including Thutmose III, Amenhotep III, Seti I, and Ramesses II, were worshipped during their lifetimes.

The cults of some kings remained active for a considerable period after their death, while others were short-lived. The cult of Thutmose I continued to thrive over five hundred years after his demise. Unfortunately, the reasons behind the varying longevity of these cults remain unknown. Some kings' cults may have been discontinued shortly after their death due to a lack of interest.

In the case of Thutmose III, the cult was probably abandoned in Dynasty 21 after rock falls from the nearby cliffs demolished his temple. However, it appears that the Egyptian situation was quite different and that the general population had a good sense of what went on inside the temple. Because these ranks of priests were drawn from the community, many men had firsthand experience with temple ritual.

He had the items that were discovered, except for those his father had already sent back to Egypt, returned to Egypt himself, during a festival and as part of his offerings, he had them transported to the temples where they had originally been removed. Although the Assyrians were famous for snatching statues from their enemies, there is little evidence to suggest that even during Ashurbanipal's conquest of Thebes in 663 BC, statues were specifically targeted. Although recycling metal statues was common among the Greeks and Romans, it is doubtful that this practice was common in Egypt. Although these smaller statues were deposited by individuals, they, like temple statues, were manifestations of Osiris and thus had been buried, returning them to the god's underworld realm.

They left one image, a statue, unmelted. For mockery, they placed it in an inappropriate location as a warning. A sad fate befell a statue that had undoubtedly garnered great reverence during its time of worship. The king was not the primary officiant for all festivals; rather, he was typically represented by a high priest. Festivals of Osiris, the god of the afterlife, were celebrated in the Karnak Temple. They were also recorded in detail at the Temple of Hathor at Dendera.

Autobiographical texts refer to individuals who participate in festivals. Duty rosters record instances where workers were required to work during a festival or granted time off to attend the celebration. Even with this amount of information, the records have a certain hollowness because we lack the more intangible aspects of festivals – the noise of people's shouts, the music, the aromas of roasting meat, incense, and perfumes; and the general atmosphere of excitement that we would recognize today. The festival is known as Khoiak, after the name of the month in which it was traditionally celebrated. The festival was not limited to the sites originally associated with the myth. However, it is known from Dendera texts that Khoiak was celebrated at Dendera, which was not one of the locations mentioned in the original myth.

The king was to play the role of Horus, the faithful son who avenge[d] the murder of Osiris. The king appointed a surrogate to represent him during festive occasions, just as the First Priest of the god acted on behalf of the king in daily temple rituals. It set out through the necropolis, led by the statue of Wepwawet, whose name meant "Opener of the Ways," and thus he was fittingly chosen to act as the protector of the god.

The public participated in the festivals of Osiris through their observation of the procession presented during the Khoiak period, as well as in more personal and intimate ways, particularly through the creation and manipulation of images of the mummified god. Corn mummies were often depicted with an erect phallus, symbolizing Osiris' ability to conceive a son after his own death. Figurines made during the Khoiak Festival were kept for a year and then ritually buried. Others became part of an individual's funerary equipment.

The figure was left in the sun, believed to magically revitalize it due to its symbolic association with new life. The figurine was returned to the temple after its outing on the sacred lake. The location of the Karnak Osiris tomb, situated outside the temple proper and near the Chapel of the Hearing Ear (Plan 1, and see Chapter 5), supports the suggestion that this was a place of more popular appeal, where community members could come during the festival of Khoiak to deposit images of the god, seeking renewal of their own lives through the promise of Osiris' rebirth.

The bricks resembled molds, but they were actually finished products, likely intended to symbolize the coffin of a god. By then, however, the festival had likely undergone a change in character, taking on more Egyptian overtones. Today's Hispanic Day of the Dead celebration shares similarities with the Feast of the Valley, as both mark festive gatherings between the living and the dead, aimed at blurring the boundaries between life and the afterlife. The festival commenced on the eastern bank of the Nile at the Karnak temple. Following purification ceremonies, the statue of Amun was removed from its dark sanctum and placed in its portable shrine. It was then loaded onto a ceremonial barge.

The procession wound its way through the ancient city's necropolis on the first day of the festival. Praising Amun, the priest Neferibed (of Dynasty 12), recorded: "Kissing the earth before the Lord of the Gods during his summer festival as he passes into the valley of Nebhepetre." "The temple's poor preservation belies its original design, which suggests a limited interior space." The sacred boat of Amun, used during the Middle Kingdom, may have been smaller than later versions. Alternatively, it is possible that the boat never entered the temple sanctuary.

During the night, Amun took on his ithyphallic form, and it was believed that he spent the night with Hathor, their union symbolizing rebirth and renewal. May what the funeral prayers call for be given to him. During the festival, participants could join in by carrying the divine boat or walking alongside it, holding the large fans that announced the divine presence. Women wore beaded necklaces and clanged metallic rattles, both instruments sacred to Hathor, as depicted in Plate III. Male musicians clapped and sang, creating a hypnotic rhythm that resonated through the tombs.

Amun's presence was celebrated into the night, as they entered Deir el-Bahri: "Praises are in heaven, jubilation is in the Great House and celebrations are on earth because Amun, in his Userhat boat, is at Djeser-Akhet [the temple of Hatshepsut]." The festival primarily centered around workers and their connection to Pharaoh Amunhotep. However, the inscriptions suggest that the women and children of these workers were also involved. The god told me to share it with you. Kenn chose to bring the dispute before the oracle of Amunhotep through the intermediary of the necropolis scribe.

Although devotees were barred from the inner sanctum of a temple, they were allowed entry, even on specific occasions, to temple courtyards and some interior spaces. The holes were often drilled through adjacent texts, suggesting that some drapery was added after the wall was originally carved. Perhaps certain reliefs developed their popularity over time. The small temple at Medinet Habu, begun during the reign of Hatshepsut and expanded upon subsequently, features an inscription from King Hakoris of Dynasty 29, which designates the site as a place for prayer and petition. Like other shrines, a Roman-era shrine at the Temple of Kom Ombo was situated on the rear exterior wall of the temple for easy and unobstructed access.

The statues of the king, like the one shown, were central to popular worship because he was seen as a mediator between humans and the divine. Votive figurines were typically offered to the gods in gratitude for divine intervention or in the expectation that the deity would intervene on behalf of the supplicant. Votive figures were a common feature throughout Egyptian history, spanning from the Early Dynastic Period to the Roman and Byzantine eras. However, their forms and materials evolved significantly over time, while their purpose remained relatively consistent.

Most were simply crafted, and while the majority were made of clay, some were of faience, which could also include baked clay or limestone. A good analogy has been drawn comparing the relative cost of a votive candle in a Catholic church to its cultural value. Similarly, the same can be said for clay votive figurines in ancient Egypt. Women seemed to be particularly active in the cults of Hathor. This might be due to their relatively lighter workload in the fields and workshops compared to men. However, it's also possible that they were more involved because Hathor was closely associated with feminine concerns such as fertility, childbirth, and love. Although the details are not entirely clear due to a lack of instruction manuals and limited depictions of people engaging in cult activities with votive figurines, it seems that votives were placed in the Hathor shrines during festivals and processions associated with the goddess.

Most female votives were depicted nude, with emphasis on the breasts and pubic area. This focus sometimes came at the expense of facial features, hands, or feet details. Initially, these figures were interpreted as "concubines for the dead." However, this conclusion seems odd, given that they were also found in the graves of women and children. A woman sat on one side of the bed, playing a lute. On the other side, a woman stood holding plucked reeds. Because votive beds have been recovered from both houses and temples, they may, like so many other types of votives, be dedicated generally to Hathor or Bes to stimulate fertility and protect the family.

Some animals, such as cats, dogs, snakes, and falcons, can be linked to cults of gods or goddesses (Sekhmet or Bastet, Sothis, Renenutet, Horus), while others remain mysterious due to their lack of apparent connection to a deity. The birds were anointed with oils and unguents in an abbreviated version of the "Opening of the Mouth" ritual, as described in Chapter 7, to revive the bird and restore its divine nature before being placed in rows within chambers carved from the rock. The Saqqara catacombs are not fully accessible, but certain areas can be explored. Those sections stretch for over 660 meters in length.

Because statues were made of stone rather than clay, they were expensive. Although limited to the wealthier segments of Egyptian society, the establishment of a statue in the temple was a common practice throughout most of the dynastic period. As pious objects and surrogates for worshippers, the statues received an honorable burial within the temple precinct.

Abydos became a place of pilgrimage due to its sacred significance. Establishing a stela at Abydos allowed the dedicator to participate in the festivals, rather than simply being a spectator. My name should not be forgotten in the future. Other dedications emphasize the significance of the statue as a recipient of food offerings brought from the main sanctuary, which were then temporarily placed beside it before being distributed to the priests.

The cult of Imhotep is best attested at Memphis and Saqqara, but only from the beginning of the New Kingdom, approximately one thousand years after his death. During the Late Period and into the Roman Period, Imhotep was associated with doctors, and he was revered as the son of Ptah. In the Late Period, nearly two thousand years after his death, Imhotep was deified and revered as a healer and as an intermediary who could convey individuals' requests to the gods.

His cult began soon after his death and continued into the Roman period. "Offer me a gift and pour a libation on my behalf, as I am the king's appointed intermediary. My role is to receive the petitions of those who come before me and report back to the king the wishes of Egypt." Figure 1 A member of the community, who apparently had been searching for an explanation for some evil events in the life of a friend or relative, recounts: "I have gone to the wise woman and she told me that the manifestation of Ptah is with you." She suggests that the individual had committed some wrongdoing noted by Ptah.

Dreams and Dreams as a Means of Contacting the Gods

The Egyptians considered sleep, especially while dreaming, to be a state of heightened awareness in another realm. This allowed the dreamer to access knowledge and beings that were distant or unknown. This event could be seen as an account of a memorable dream, had it not been for Ipuy's statement, "It was on the day that I saw her beauty," indicating that he was awake, not asleep. We have a few texts that reflect a special devotion to a single god, or an almost fanatical attachment, which may foreshadow true monasticism.

Ordinary people were not allowed to enter the temples' sanctuaries, but other areas were designated for public use and gatherings. While divine-human encounters described in the previous chapter were often positive, unsolicited meetings with a god could be a frightening event, serving as a warning of the deity's impending wrath due to some personal misstep or shortcoming. Most people experience such situations due to unfortunate events and feelings of guilt, leading them to confess their wrongdoing to the gods, who are appeased, enabling the individual to make amends. The frequency of texts referring to the use of oracles might be due to several factors. Firstly, a greater number of texts from that period have survived, which could contribute to this phenomenon. Secondly, certain types of routine matters, particularly those related to property, were often taken to the oracle rather than the court.

During the Opet Festival, the gods made their journey from Karnak to the Luxor Temple. As I waited, I met Hori, a scribe from the temple of Ramesses III. He told me, "I have access."  The gods were said to become "heavy" in other examples. The priests who supported the bow of the boat were pressed down by the will of the god. The oracular process may seem farcical from a modern perspective, but it is evident that those directly involved and observers genuinely believed the movement of the sacred boat and its accompanying priests signified the divine will.

Conflict of interest in allowing priests who bore the oracle to present their own petitions was avoided by forbidding them from submitting petitions while on duty. While most oracular texts refer to the god's decision being conveyed through movement, some also mention other modes of communication. The practice conflicted with Roman law, leading to several edicts that sought to limit its use. The Egyptian gods were generally benevolent and approachable, but they also had a darker, more malevolent aspect.

There was once a king who, due to his unique connection with the gods, could command the forces of the gods against his enemies. The Hittite Marriage of Ramesses II records an instance where the power of Seth was upon the Hittites, as they failed to show reverence for the Egyptian king. In another literary example, it is stated that since the Hittites refused to submit to Egyptian power, Ramses II sent the army of Seth against them, resulting in a famine. One stela neatly illustrates how a god's influence can force individuals to reconsider their actions.

They will also be passed down to their children's children . A woman who stole bread during the festival of the birth of Taweret felt the "divine anger" of the goddess. The threat of being under the yoke of a god was a frightening proposition.

Behold your place, seize it for yourself. While I slept and the earth lay in silence at night's depths, my heart was jubilant in the morning. I rejoiced and went to the western side of the river to fulfill what you had instructed. As you had said, so I did.

We claim that we will protect her from those who bring about evil states of affairs, even though there is no such thing as an evil state of affairs. These and other types of amulets could defend against various mishaps, including miscarriage, reptile bites and stings from crocodiles and scorpions, accidents while riding in a horse-drawn vehicle, traveling by boat, or walking along the riverbank. They also protected against thunderbolts and collapsing walls.

She may make a case for Seth against me. During the Third Intermediate Period, theophoric names became increasingly common, particularly those referring to the protection or patronage of a specific god. Examples include Djed-khonsu-iwes-ankh ("Khonsu Said That She Will Live") and Pay-chaw-em-awy-Amun ("His Air [is] Amun"). e Is in the hands of Amun. Ptah-irr is the one who made him. Even in those examples, religious practices like the wearing of amulets offered relief and protection, reinforcing the idea that the universe is fundamentally fair.

It comes to those who are on earth. As with other unknown phenomena, the Egyptians developed a conception of life after death rooted in their observations and experiences. Life after death was not significantly different from life itself. Existence was simply transferred to another, more remote realm. The proximity and familiarity of the afterlife were comforting because the routines there provided answers about the unknowable in terms that were entirely understandable. Those grieving the loss of a family member could imagine where the deceased was, and even in their grief, they could find some comfort in knowing what life after death might be like for them.

They claimed to have remained unmarried out of loyalty to their late wives' memories. The Egyptians' mortuary theology was based on the idea that all those who lived their lives morally would be reborn in the afterlife. Regardless of their relative cost, they were considered to be equally effective for serving the deceased in the afterlife. They who were less fortunate found solace in the knowledge that in the afterlife, they would be on par with the wealthy elite whose lavish lifestyles and status they likely envied.

Because a deceased person's physical needs do not change with death, they would require shelter in the form of a tomb, food and drink, pleasurable activities, and the comforts of everyday life. Some tombs were grouped together based on shared professions. If this occurred after the tomb had been abandoned by its original owner's family, it could be refurbished.

A man named Djau recalled why he had chosen to share the tomb of his father, also named Djau: "I wanted to honor my father's memory by sharing our family's history and legacy with others." Djau Sr was buried in one tomb along with his family. They were together in one place, not because they lacked the resources to build another tomb. "Many tombs, perhaps the majority, were incomplete at the time of their owner's death." They could stand back and marvel at their work.

Debehren (Dynasty 4) described all the finished details of his tomb, including its false door, limestone walls, and statues, giving the impression that his tomb was complete. Once the tomb was completed, it stood open, ready for burial. The offering chapel was a semipublic space intended for visitation by the living. It featured doors that controlled access. The entrance to the burial chamber was sealed with a mud-brick or stone wall after the funeral, rendering it inaccessible to all but the most determined thieves.

Those from the tomb of Tutankhamun were topped with wooden statues. Other, lesser tombs were protected by crudely made magic bricks with hastily drawn or incised hieratic inscriptions. These bricks are known to have existed during the New Kingdom period, up to Dynasty 21. They then disappeared for an unknown period before reappearing in the Late Period. The burial chamber was sealed after the deposit of the coffin and grave goods.

The interior of the wooden coffin of Ipi-ha-ishutef was adorned with paintings depicting clothing, food, and everyday objects that the deceased had desired for the afterlife. Because the deceased lived on in the afterlife, these offerings had to be provided for eternity. However, the Egyptians recognized that, at some point, the arrangements for leaving food would break down as family resources were redirected towards other needs, or the memory of the individual faded over time, and the family moved away. The concern shown for providing for the offering cult is reflected in economic records from all periods, which established funds to pay for the offerings after the tomb owner's death.

The official Tjenti even stated that he begged the land from the king. Alongside the lists of what was desired for the offering cults, there were also legal texts that aimed to safeguard the sources of those offerings. These offerings were originally referred to as "voice offerings" (peret kherw), literally meaning "what goes forth at the voice." The act of pronouncing the names of the offerings along with the name of the deceased brought them into being in the afterlife, exemplifying the efficiency and economy of Egyptian rituals. The number of days was based on the astronomical phenomenon of decans, stars that remained below the horizon for a period of 70 days before rising above it. This reference alludes to the ancient Egyptian belief in the resurrection of the deceased from the afterlife.

There was significant variation in the actual timing of these objects. Some scholars consider them to be offering tables, but their rectangular shape and slant towards the drain and basin suggest an association with embalming. Their function is unknown, but they may have been used to position limbs during the wrapping process.

The Egyptians regarded the mummy as an imperishable image of the deceased. Although it was the intention for people to be mummified, not everyone could afford this luxury. Hatnofer, the father of Senenmut, a high official of Hatshepsut, who could certainly have afforded to be mummified, was not. Funerary stelae often include the statement, "It is his son who sustains his name," a reference to the proper commemoration of the deceased, ensuring a proper afterlife.

A man wrote a letter complaining that he had dutifully buried his brother, despite the fact that the deceased had owed him linen and thirty measures of grain. The funeral one of the most moving descriptions of an ancient funeral is recorded in a text found in the tomb of Dheuty at Thebes. A beautiful burial, may it come in peace, after your seventy days have been completed in your embalming hall. May the chief lector priest open your mouth, may the sem-priest purify you, and may Horus weigh your heart for you after he has revealed your eyes and ears to you. After the ceremonial seventy days required to prepare the corpse, the body was finally released.

The coffin was accompanied by a smaller shrine containing a compartmentalized box for the canopic jars, which held the desiccated organs: lungs, liver, stomach, and intestines, removed during the mummification process. The New Kingdom texts refer to the ferry as the neshmet barque, the sacred boat of Osiris, an allusion to his association with the god of the afterlife. A priest recited the spells of the Opening of the Mouth ceremony, as he held up a ceremonial knife. Two other priests stood beside him, one holding a vessel of water and the other a container of natron, which they used to purify the mummy with incense and liquids.

A clay mask in the form of Anubis, the guardian of the necropolis, may have been worn by priests during funerary ceremonies. This is a rare example of such a mask, and not all scholars agree that they were worn during rituals. One detailed account from the Ramessid period (circa 1100 BC) describes how the craftsmen who accompanied the funeral described the various forms of their "father" to the priest after seeing them.

The foreleg was a particularly appropriate symbolic offering because it shares the same form as the hieroglyph for "power" and also resembles the adze used by the sem priest in the ritual. At the conclusion of the ritual, the priests recited a summary of the rituals and their efficacy for the deceased: I have given breath to those who are in hiding, I have enabled those who are in the underworld to breathe. I have caused them to rest in their chapels and their offerings to endure. The breath of life comes and creates his image; his mouth is opened. Amunhotep IV initiated a new theology during his reign, one that redefined the nature of the world and the relationship between the king and his subjects with the divine.

The temples of other gods were not officially closed. State financial support for the other cults was transferred to the cult of the Aten. The most direct action was taken against the gods who dwelled in the great temples of Karnak and Luxor in ancient Thebes. This may have been due to the close connection between the rituals performed there and the traditional concept of kingship. Akhenaten apparently died after 17 years on the throne.

Theology sought to condense the intricate realm of multiple deities and the elaborate explanations for life and existence beyond death, as outlined in the Book of the Dead and other collections of mortal theology, into a single, unified concept: all life originates from the sun god Aten. In contrast to other Egyptian deities, the Aten had no consort. However, it did have one son, King Akhena, also known as Waenre, who was referred to as "the one whom the Aten had begotten" or "your beautiful son." This son was considered the beloved child of the Aten, and his birth is often associated with the Aten's role as a creator deity. "The god itself was said to have come from nothingness. It was stated, 'Who made himself?' and 'O God... it is you who were made when there was no creator.'" Another major difference between the Aten and the traditional gods was its relationship to humans. In contrast to earlier texts, it was stated that one could not "know the god." In the Amarna theology, however, it was said that "one cannot know" the Aten, as this relationship was reserved exclusively for the king. During the Amarna Period, individuals lacked direct connection with the deity as the path of communication with the Aten passed through and concluded at the royal court.

One's desired fate - to pass into a happy afterlife - was determined by an individual's moral behavior while alive. The conception of the afterlife in the Amarna period differed significantly from traditional theology, which had developed gradually over a millennium and a half. In contrast, the Amarna doctrine emerged rapidly over several years, potentially contributing to its incomplete treatment of various aspects of life and death. The concept of an afterlife is a notable deficiency in theology, particularly when compared to traditional religious beliefs.

Individuals believed they had control over their lives, which influenced their perception of rebirth. The realm of the dead during the Amarna Period was a desolate place devoid of the Aten's radiance. In the Amarna Period, the afterlife revolved around the king, for he dispensed life after death. May your corpse be firm, may your name last forever. May you breathe the air of the northern winds.

The tomb scenes reveal that an individual's identity was no longer paramount in their afterlife. Instead, eternal existence was centered on the royal family, who held the power to dispense eternity. After death, the ba, representing an individual's energy as a human-headed bird, was believed to leave the tomb at dawn and reunite with the living, often depicted sitting in trees and quenching its thirst from canals.

The incorporation of many people into the priesthood in a well-ordered manner, with clearly defined duties, may have led to a greater sense of community involvement and the creation of an additional network of social support through their interconnected responsibilities. The choice of this title was likely intended to be fitting due to the solar nature of the Aten.

There are fewer types of priests attested at Amarna. Perhaps this is because the god communicated only with the king, eliminating the need for a priestly hierarchy. This is especially true since the elaborate daily offering service had been replaced by the simple offering of flowers and food on the offering tables of the Aten temples. They were forced to abandon one of the most advanced cities in the world, a metropolis renowned for its unparalleled sophistication, which was nicknamed "The City" due to its unmatched excellence. The city's isolation and lack of development made it an ideal location for a new settlement. It replaced a sense of pride in moral behavior with a blind loyalty to the king, who was seen as the sole arbiter of one's fate.

The focus of the movement was entirely on the king – he was the sole communicant with the Aten, and it was he who granted health and an afterlife. Some texts refer to Osiris, while others substitute the name Aten. There are only a few hints, including some from Akhenaten's reign, and more direct indications of dissent following his death.

The ancient temple's restoration inscription vividly described the deplorable state of its structures, which had transformed into pathways, and their sacred sanctuaries were now overgrown with wild grass. It is perhaps a mark of the greater utility and appeal of traditional beliefs that they have endured so rapidly after the death of Akhenaten. Afterword: An Appraisal of Egyptian Religion

The preceding chapters have sought to demonstrate how ancient Egyptian religious beliefs and practices operated within the community, shaping patterns of human behavior.

However, it is not just the religion's creativity and uniqueness that are striking, but also its longevity. The comforting aspect of religious beliefs must have been widespread because most cult practices were easily accessible. Although one could lavish immense resources on commissioning a stone statue for dedication in the temple, a statue of inferior materials and workmanship was deemed to be as effective.

This equality before the gods is reinforced by texts that state that it was proper behavior, not worldly goods, that determined a person's fate. The concept of life after death is rooted in recognizing what life is like and then extending it beyond death, or by observing the cyclical nature of birth, death, and rebirth, as seen in the sun's eternal cycle. More detailed knowledge about the transition from life to the afterlife was provided by spells from the Book of the Dead or other texts that were the domain of the high elite. However, these spells were merely embellishments and not essential components of the belief.

The evidence suggests that Egyptian religion was widely accepted by society due to its ability to evoke excitement and joy. Although many aspects of ancient Egyptian religion appear exotic and mysterious today, they were, in the context of the ancient society, reasonable and functional manifestations of a belief system that provided a complex and enduring network of support for individuals and society.
